title: 09_truncated_answers.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Stop Losing Answers Before Training Starts

The support team is fine-tuning DistilBERT for extractive QA over internal tickets: given an agent's question and a ticket's resolution notes, the model must point at the exact character span that answers it. Preprocessing tokenizes each question/context pair; this notebook is the data-audit gate that must pass before any fine-tune launches. It re-tokenizes every training example, maps the labeled answer span through the offset mapping, and asserts that fewer than 1% of answers fall outside the encoded input.

The gate is deep red: 38% of training answers vanish during tokenization. Nothing crashes — the tokenizer happily returns a tidy, valid encoding for every single example. But a span-extraction model cannot learn to find an answer that its own input pipeline already discarded. Work out where the answers are going, and make every labeled span survive encoding.
initialCode:
import json

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
examples = [json.loads(line) for line in open("support_qa.jsonl")]

def answer_survives(ex):
    enc = tok(ex["question"], ex["context"], max_length=64,
              truncation=True, return_offsets_mapping=True)
    ctx_ends = [off[1] for off, sid in zip(enc["offset_mapping"], enc.sequence_ids())
                if sid == 1]
    answer_end = ex["answer_start"] + len(ex["answer_text"])
    return answer_end <= max(ctx_ends, default=0)

lost = sum(not answer_survives(ex) for ex in examples)
rate = lost / len(examples)
print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"
traceback:
answers lost to truncation: 190/500 (38%)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 rate = lost / len(examples)
     18 print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
---> 19 assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"

AssertionError: 38% of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)

options.label: Raise max_length to the model's 512-token ceiling and keep truncation on
options.patchCode:
import json

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
examples = [json.loads(line) for line in open("support_qa.jsonl")]

def answer_survives(ex):
    enc = tok(ex["question"], ex["context"], max_length=512,
              truncation=True, return_offsets_mapping=True)
    ctx_ends = [off[1] for off, sid in zip(enc["offset_mapping"], enc.sequence_ids())
                if sid == 1]
    answer_end = ex["answer_start"] + len(ex["answer_text"])
    return answer_end <= max(ctx_ends, default=0)

lost = sum(not answer_survives(ex) for ex in examples)
rate = lost / len(examples)
print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"
options.resultLog:
answers lost to truncation: 14/500 (3%)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 19
     17 rate = lost / len(examples)
     18 print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
---> 19 assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"

AssertionError: 3% of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)
options.rationale: Going from 64 to the model's 512-token ceiling rescues most spans, but it is still one fixed window with no overflow handling: the 14 longest tickets blow past 512 tokens and their answers are discarded exactly as silently as before. There is a subtler problem too — `truncation=True` means `longest_first`, which trims whichever sequence is currently longer, so a verbose question can also be nibbled. Any single-window budget is a bet that no document will ever exceed it; windowing with a stride makes that bet unnecessary.

options.label: Right-size max_length and window long contexts with truncation='only_second' plus a stride
options.patchCode:
import json

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
examples = [json.loads(line) for line in open("support_qa.jsonl")]

def answer_survives(ex):
    enc = tok(ex["question"], ex["context"], max_length=384,
              truncation="only_second", stride=128,
              return_overflowing_tokens=True, return_offsets_mapping=True)
    answer_end = ex["answer_start"] + len(ex["answer_text"])
    for i, offsets in enumerate(enc["offset_mapping"]):
        ctx = [off for off, sid in zip(offsets, enc.sequence_ids(i)) if sid == 1]
        if ctx and ctx[0][0] <= ex["answer_start"] and answer_end <= ctx[-1][1]:
            return True
    return False

lost = sum(not answer_survives(ex) for ex in examples)
rate = lost / len(examples)
print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"
options.rationale: `truncation="only_second"` declares the question untouchable and names the context as the only sequence that may be cut, and `return_overflowing_tokens=True` with `stride=128` stops cutting altogether: each long pair becomes several overlapping 384-token windows instead of one amputated encoding. Because consecutive windows share 128 tokens of context, any answer span shorter than the overlap is guaranteed to land fully inside at least one window — the audit walks the windows with `sequence_ids(i)` and finds every span. 384/128 are the battle-tested SQuAD fine-tuning defaults, and at training time each window simply becomes its own feature, with windows that miss the answer labeled no-answer.

options.label: Filter the training set down to examples whose answers already survive tokenization
options.patchCode:
import json

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("distilbert-base-uncased")
examples = [json.loads(line) for line in open("support_qa.jsonl")]

def answer_survives(ex):
    enc = tok(ex["question"], ex["context"], max_length=64,
              truncation=True, return_offsets_mapping=True)
    ctx_ends = [off[1] for off, sid in zip(enc["offset_mapping"], enc.sequence_ids())
                if sid == 1]
    answer_end = ex["answer_start"] + len(ex["answer_text"])
    return answer_end <= max(ctx_ends, default=0)

examples = [ex for ex in examples if answer_survives(ex)]

lost = sum(not answer_survives(ex) for ex in examples)
rate = lost / len(examples)
print(f"answers lost to truncation: {lost}/{len(examples)} ({rate:.0%})")
assert rate < 0.01, f"{rate:.0%} of training answers truncated away (limit 1%)"
options.rationale: The gate turns green by destroying the evidence: 190 of the 500 examples — the long-context tickets, the exact cases the support team needs answered — are deleted instead of fixed. The model now trains exclusively on answers that sit within the first ~50 tokens of short documents, so at serving time it will be systematically blind to anything buried deep in a real ticket. An audit exists to measure the pipeline, not to be satisfied by curating its input; this is the data-engineering equivalent of deleting a failing test.

hints: Tokenizers never refuse oversized input — they cut it to fit and hand back a perfectly normal-looking encoding. In extractive QA the training label is a *position inside* that encoding. Ask what happens to a label whose position no longer exists after encoding.

Lines 9–10: `max_length=64` with `truncation=True` (an alias for `longest_first`) leaves roughly 50 tokens of context once the question and special tokens take their share — everything past that is silently dropped, including 38% of the answer spans. The tokenizer offers a strategy that only ever cuts the second sequence, plus a companion pair of arguments that turn one long context into overlapping windows instead of cutting it at all.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`truncation=True` with `max_length=64` orders the tokenizer to make every question/context pair fit in 64 tokens, whatever it must discard to get there. After `[CLS]`, the question, and the separator tokens take their share, roughly 50 tokens of budget remain for the context — and support tickets routinely run hundreds of tokens. The default `longest_first` strategy drops everything beyond the budget and returns a clean, valid encoding: no exception, no warning. For classification that would merely lose signal; for extractive QA the label *is* a position in the context, so when the audit maps each `answer_start`/`answer_text` through the offset mapping, 38% of spans point past the last surviving context token. Training would teach the model to predict positions its inputs no longer contain.

### Why the fix is correct
Two changes restore the invariant that every label survives preprocessing. `truncation="only_second"` makes the question inviolable and names the context as the only cuttable sequence — the correct semantics for QA pairs. And instead of cutting, `return_overflowing_tokens=True` with `stride=128` re-encodes each long context as a series of 384-token windows overlapping by 128 tokens, so any answer span shorter than the overlap necessarily falls completely inside at least one window. Each window becomes its own training feature (windows that miss the answer get the no-answer label), the audit verifies coverage per window via `sequence_ids(i)` and the offset mapping, and zero spans are lost. The principle: when a transform is lossy, either bound the loss and prove the bound, or restructure the transform so the labels cannot be lost.
recruiterReview: Solid work — you treated the tokenizer as what it is, a lossy transform, and fixed the loss instead of hiding it. The principle I push on every data team is exactly this: labels must provably survive preprocessing, because no optimizer can recover information the pipeline destroyed before the first gradient step. You also dodged both traps — a bigger fixed window just relocates the cliff, and filtering out the failures trains a model that is blind to precisely the documents production will throw at it. Forward-looking: derive max_length from the token-length distribution of real tickets rather than by convention, log windows-per-example so cost regressions stay visible, and keep this coverage audit as a permanent CI gate in front of every fine-tune. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## The Tokenizer Cuts Before It Complains

Every transformer consumes a fixed-length token window, and the tokenizer's job is to make arbitrary text fit it. `truncation=True` is not safety equipment — it is a standing order to discard whatever does not fit, silently. The encoding that comes back is perfectly well-formed: right length, right special tokens, valid attention mask. Nothing about it announces "I threw away 60% of your document." That silence seeds an entire family of data bugs, and the damage is worst in tasks where the *label lives inside the input*.

### Span labels die in truncation
Classification degrades gracefully under truncation: lose the tail of a document, lose some signal. Span tasks — extractive QA, NER, evidence highlighting — fail absolutely. If the answer starts at character 1,900 and the surviving context covers 300 characters, the example's label points at nothing. Depending on how the label-conversion code handles it, the example either crashes the pipeline, gets a garbage token index, or is quietly relabeled "no answer" — actively teaching the model that long documents never contain answers. None of this is visible until you measure it: re-tokenize the dataset, map every character-level label through `offset_mapping`, and count how many survive.

### Truncation strategies are not interchangeable
For paired inputs the strategy decides *what* gets cut, and the choices differ sharply:
- `True` / `"longest_first"` — removes tokens from whichever sequence is currently longer. Convenient for sentence pairs, hazardous for QA: a verbose question competes with the context for budget.
- `"only_second"` — the first sequence is protected; only the second may be cut. For (question, context) pairs this is almost always what you mean.
- `"only_first"` — the mirror image, for tasks where the second sequence is sacred.
Picking a strategy deliberately is the difference between losing filler and losing labels.

### Sliding windows: don't cut, chunk
When the second sequence genuinely exceeds the window, the right move is not a bigger window but `return_overflowing_tokens=True` with a `stride`. The tokenizer emits a series of encodings whose contexts overlap by `stride` tokens, so any span shorter than the overlap is guaranteed to sit entirely inside at least one window. Each window becomes its own training feature: the question is repeated, character labels are re-mapped per window through `offset_mapping` and `sequence_ids()`, and windows that miss the span get the no-answer label. At inference you score all windows and aggregate the best span. The classic SQuAD recipe — `max_length=384`, `stride=128` — exists because it makes coverage a mathematical guarantee instead of a hope.

### Audit coverage like a release gate
Size the window from data, not habit: tokenize the corpus once and look at the length percentiles before choosing `max_length`. Then keep a coverage assert in CI — spans found after tokenization divided by spans labeled — so the next person who "simplifies" the preprocessing gets a red build instead of a quietly crippled model. Track windows-per-example too; stride trades compute for coverage, and that cost should be visible.

### Common variations of this bug
- Sentiment or topic classifiers whose decisive sentence sits past the truncation point — accuracy silently capped by preprocessing, not by the model.
- NER pipelines where entity character offsets beyond the window are dropped or, worse, misaligned during label-to-token conversion.
- `longest_first` shaving tokens off the *question* when it competes with a long context for the budget.
- Chat or dialogue models truncating from the right when the newest turns matter most — generative stacks often need `truncation_side="left"`.
- Train/serve skew: fine-tuning with one `max_length` and serving with another, so the model meets position distributions it never saw.
- Sizing the budget in words instead of tokens — subword tokenizers can triple the length of domain text full of IDs, codes, and URLs.
